title: Suppression of p-Wave Altermagnetism by Localized 4f Electrons in CeNiAsO
authors: Jiuxiang Zhang, Yueyang Sun, Honglin Zhou, Jumin Shi, Di Wu, Hongze Gu, Wenjin Mao, Hengrui Dong, Yu Xu, Yinghao Li, Ziling Cao, Taimin Miao, Bo Liang, Neng Cai, Wenpei Zhu, Mingkai Xu, Jiaqi Chen, Chunhong Deng, Bo Liu, Xun Ma, Zhengtai Liu, Mao Ye, Shenjin Zhang, Zhimin Wang, Fengfeng Zhang, Feng Yang, Qinjun Peng, Zuyan Xu, Guodong Liu, Xintong Li, Hanqing Mao, Shiliang Li, Hongming Weng, Lin Zhao, X. J. Zhou
abstract: Altermagnetism, characterized by momentum-dependent spin splitting and zero net magnetization, has so far been explored mainly in weakly or moderately correlated d-electron systems. How such symmetry-allowed band splitting manifests in heavy-fermion materials, where magnetic exchange competes with Kondo correlations, remains unclear. Here we use high-resolution angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy to investigate CeNiAsO, a heavy-fermion candidate for p-wave altermagnetism. Despite macroscopic signatures consistent with the proposed p-wave magnetic order, we find no resolvable near-Fermi-level p-wave exchange splitting on the Ni 3d-derived conduction bands across the Neel transitions. Fermi-surface mapping, orbital-resolved ARPES identify that the low-energy electronic structure is dominated by Ni 3d bands, while resonant photoemission reveals that the Ce 4f states remain predominantly localized with residual c-f hybridization. First-principles calculations further show that an uncorrected itinerant-4f description produces dispersive Ce 4f bands and additional Fermi-surface pockets that are absent in experiment, thereby overestimating both the low-energy c-f hybridization and the exchange splitting transferred to the Ni 3d bands. When the localized Ce 4f character is incorporated through DFT+U , the experimental Fermi-surface topology is recovered and the residual p-wave splitting on the Ni 3d-derived bands is reduced to only a few meV, below the effective experimental resolution. These results identify CeNiAsO as a strongly correlated f-electron limit of p-wave magnetism, in which localized 4f electrons suppress the observable single-particle band-splitting signature expected from a weak-correlation picture.
summary: This work investigates p-wave altermagnetism in the heavy-fermion material CeNiAsO using ARPES and DFT calculations. The key finding is that strong correlations from localized Ce 4f electrons suppress the expected single-particle band-splitting signature. This suggests that the material operates in a strongly correlated limit where simple weak-coupling theories fail to describe the magnetic electronic structure.
main_problem: To understand how symmetry-allowed p-wave altermagnetism manifests in heavy-fermion materials where magnetic exchange competes with Kondo correlations.
main_result: Strong $4f$ correlations, when treated correctly using DFT+U, suppress the observable single-particle band-splitting signature expected from a weak-correlation picture of p-wave magnetism.
method: The study combines high-resolution Angle-Resolved Photoemission Spectroscopy (ARPES) measurements with first-principles calculations using DFT, comparing itinerant vs. localized $4f$ electron treatments.
model_or_system: The system studied is CeNiAsO, a heavy-fermion candidate for p-wave altermagnetism, involving the interplay between Ni $3d$ conduction bands and localized Ce $4f$ magnetic moments.
key_observables: Resolvable near-Fermi-level p-wave exchange splitting on Ni $3d$ bands, Fermi-surface topology, and the spectral weight of Ce $4f$ states.
important_parameters: The comparison between uncorrected itinerant-4f description and the localized-4f treatment ($U_{	ext{eff}}$) is critical; the residual splitting is found to be in the few-meV range.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the strong $4f$ correlations fundamentally alter the single-particle spectral signature of the altermagnetic order, making the weak-correlation picture inapplicable.
figures_summary: Figures detail the crystal structure, magnetic susceptibility, ARPES Fermi-surface mappings, and critically compare calculated band structures using different $U_{	ext{eff}}$ values to show the suppression of splitting.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the experimental context using ARPES to probe the magnetic state. It then uses DFT calculations, contrasting itinerant vs. localized $4f$ treatments, to theoretically explain the observed suppression of the expected p-wave splitting.
why_interesting: The concept of correlation-driven suppression of magnetic signatures is highly relevant to understanding exotic quantum states in strongly correlated materials, bridging magnetism and electronic structure theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
